\documentclass{article}

\usepackage{amsmath}
\usepackage{amsthm}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}

\begin{document}

\title{A Toy Fixed-Point Note}
\author{Test Author}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
We show that for all continuous functions $g : [0, 1] \to [0, 1]$ the iteration
$x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ converges to a unique fixed point from any starting value.
The argument is elementary toy material.
\end{abstract}

\section{A Convergence Result}
Consider a map $g : [0, 1] \to [0, 1]$.

\begin{theorem}
\label{thm:contraction}
Suppose $g : [0, 1] \to [0, 1]$ is Lipschitz with constant $\kappa < 1$, i.e.
$|g(x) - g(y)| \le \kappa |x - y|$ for all $x, y \in [0, 1]$. Then $g$ has a
unique fixed point $x^\star$, and for any $x_0 \in [0, 1]$ the iteration
$x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ satisfies $|x_n - x^\star| \le \kappa^n |x_0 - x^\star| \to 0$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
For any $x, y$, $|g(x) - g(y)| \le \kappa |x - y|$ with $\kappa < 1$, so $g$ is a
contraction on the complete metric space $[0, 1]$. By the Banach fixed-point
theorem $g$ has a unique fixed point $x^\star$, and iterating the contraction
estimate gives $|x_n - x^\star| = |g(x_{n-1}) - g(x^\star)| \le \kappa |x_{n-1} -
x^\star| \le \cdots \le \kappa^n |x_0 - x^\star|$, which tends to $0$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
